title: Topic 112: Why Subscription Models Feel Different: The Netflix-ification of Gaming
content:
## ABSTRACT

This paper navigates the treacherous, snack-filled waters of the modern gaming landscape, a world increasingly dominated by the "all-you-can-eat" buffet of subscription services. We chart the course of the "Netflix-ification" of gaming, exploring how the industry has shifted from the satisfying ownership of a single, well-chosen steak dinner to an endless parade of digital appetizers. Through a rigorous, yet hilarious, analysis of consumer psychology, we examine the paradox of choice, the illusion of ownership, and the insidious "just one more level" mentality that these services foster. By dissecting real-world examples like the benevolent dictatorship of Xbox Game Pass and the existential crisis of a dusty game cartridge, we reveal the profound and often comical ways in which subscription models are rewiring our brains and reshaping our relationship with the very concept of play. Ultimately, we ask the question: are we players in a game, or just subscribers to a service?

## INTRODUCTION

Remember the sacred ritual? The pilgrimage to the local video game store, the careful weighing of box art and back-of-the-box promises, the triumphant return home with a plastic-wrapped treasure. Remember the satisfying heft of a new cartridge, the ceremonial blowing of dust from its connectors (a practice of dubious efficacy but unquestionable spiritual importance), and the singular focus of conquering a single, monolithic game? If you do, you might be a gaming geriatric, a relic of a bygone era before the great digital flood. Today, the gaming experience is less about a cherished possession and more about an endless, scrolling menu. We have entered the age of the "Netflix-ification" of gaming, a world where our libraries are not curated collections but bottomless pits of content, beckoning us with the siren song of "just one more." This paper argues that while the subscription model offers unprecedented access to a vast universe of games, it also fundamentally alters our relationship with them, transforming them from treasured artifacts into disposable entertainment. We will embark on a journey through the psychology of this new paradigm, from the paralyzing anxiety of the paradox of choice to the phantom limb syndrome of digital ownership. We will dissect the business models that have turned our consoles into content treadmills and explore the real-world implications for both gamers and the industry at large. So, grab your controllers, settle into your gaming chairs, and prepare to question everything you thought you knew about the simple act of play.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The subscription model is not a new phenomenon, of course. For decades, we've subscribed to everything from magazines about cats to monthly boxes of artisanal pickles. But the digital age has supercharged this model, with services like Netflix and Spotify transforming how we consume movies and music. The academic world, ever eager to dissect the mundane, has produced a wealth of research on the psychology of subscriptions. Scholars have explored the "sunk cost fallacy," where we continue to pay for a service we don't use simply because we've already invested in it [1]. They've also delved into the murky waters of "subscription inertia," the baffling tendency of consumers to keep paying for services they've forgotten they even have [2].

In the gaming world, the subscription model has been a game-changer, quite literally. Research from Van Crombrugge and Stremersch (2025) suggests that services like Xbox Game Pass and PlayStation Plus have had a largely positive impact on the industry, boosting console sales and creating a healthier supply of games [3]. This stands in stark contrast to the music and movie industries, where subscription models have been accused of cannibalizing sales and devaluing content. The key difference, it seems, is the interactive nature of games. While you can passively consume a movie or a song, a game demands your active participation, creating a deeper sense of engagement and, potentially, a greater willingness to pay.

But what of the gamer's psyche? How does this all-you-can-eat buffet of interactive entertainment affect our brains? The literature on the "paradox of choice" suggests that an overabundance of options can lead to anxiety, indecision, and ultimately, dissatisfaction [4]. With hundreds of games at our fingertips, we are faced with a tyranny of choice. Do we spend our precious free time meticulously researching the perfect game, or do we just endlessly scroll through the menu, paralyzed by the sheer volume of content? This is the digital equivalent of standing in front of a refrigerator packed with food and declaring, "There's nothing to eat!"

## ANALYSIS

The Netflix-ification of gaming is more than just a shift in business models; it's a fundamental rewiring of the gamer's brain. At the heart of this transformation lies the **paradox of choice**, a psychological phenomenon that turns the promise of infinite entertainment into a source of existential dread. With a library of hundreds of games at their disposal, the modern gamer is less like a kid in a candy store and more like a deer in the headlights of an oncoming content truck. The sheer volume of options can lead to a state of perpetual browsing, where more time is spent deciding what to play than actually playing. This is the dark side of abundance: when everything is available, nothing feels special.

This sense of disposability is further amplified by the **illusion of ownership**. Unlike the tangible satisfaction of a physical game cartridge, a digital license is a fleeting and ephemeral thing. We don't own the games in our subscription libraries; we merely rent them. This creates a psychological distance, a lack of attachment that makes it easier to abandon a game at the first sign of difficulty or boredom. The days of doggedly mastering a single, challenging game are over. In the age of Game Pass, we are all digital nomads, wandering from one game to the next, never truly putting down roots.

The subscription model also masterfully exploits the **'just one more' mentality**, a behavioral loop that has long been the bread and butter of the gaming industry. By removing the friction of individual purchases, subscription services make it dangerously easy to keep playing. The 'sunk cost' of the monthly fee encourages us to maximize our investment, to squeeze every last drop of content out of the service. This can lead to a form of digital gluttony, where we consume games not out of genuine enjoyment, but out of a sense of obligation. We are no longer playing for fun; we are playing to get our money's worth.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

To truly understand the Netflix-ification of gaming, we must venture into the wild and observe these subscription services in their natural habitat. Our first specimen is the undisputed king of the digital jungle: **Xbox Game Pass**. Microsoft's subscription service has been hailed as the "best deal in gaming," and for good reason. For a monthly fee, subscribers get access to a vast and rotating library of games, including brand-new releases from Microsoft's own studios. This has been a boon for both gamers and indie developers, who have found a new platform to showcase their work. However, the Game Pass model also raises questions about the long-term sustainability of the industry. If gamers are no longer buying games outright, how will developers fund their next ambitious project? Are we creating a generation of gamers who are unwilling to pay full price for a premium experience?

Next, we turn our attention to **PlayStation Plus**, Sony's answer to Game Pass. While Microsoft has gone all-in on the "all-you-can-eat" model, Sony has taken a more cautious approach, offering a tiered system with different levels of access. This reflects a fundamental difference in philosophy. Microsoft sees Game Pass as a way to attract new players to the Xbox ecosystem, while Sony is more focused on rewarding its existing user base. The PlayStation Plus model is less disruptive than Game Pass, but it also feels less revolutionary. It's like a buffet that charges extra for the crab legs.

Finally, we must pour one out for the fallen soldiers of the subscription wars, the services that promised the world and delivered a dial-up connection. Remember **Google Stadia**? The cloud-based gaming service that was supposed to revolutionize the industry but ended up as a punchline. Stadia's failure serves as a cautionary tale. It's not enough to simply offer a library of games; you also need the infrastructure, the user base, and a clear value proposition. The road to the Netflix-ification of gaming is littered with the corpses of failed experiments, a testament to the difficulty of changing consumer habits.

## DISCUSSION

The Netflix-ification of gaming is not simply a new way to sell games; it's a paradigm shift that is reshaping the very foundations of the industry. The long-term implications of this transformation are still unfolding, but we can already see the fault lines forming. For developers, the subscription model offers a new revenue stream and a way to reach a wider audience, but it also creates a pressure to design games that are "sub-friendly" – experiences that are easily digestible and endlessly replayable. This could lead to a homogenization of content, a world where every game is a rogue-like or a live service, designed to keep players on the digital treadmill for as long as possible.

For gamers, the subscription model is a double-edged sword. On the one hand, it offers an unprecedented level of access and affordability. On the other hand, it fosters a culture of digital gluttony and disposable entertainment. The challenge for the modern gamer is to navigate this new landscape with intention and mindfulness, to resist the siren song of the endless backlog and to cultivate a deeper appreciation for the games they choose to play. This may require a conscious effort to set limits, to curate our own digital libraries, and to remember that the value of a game is not determined by its price tag.

The lessons of the Netflix-ification of gaming extend far beyond the world of interactive entertainment. As more and more industries embrace the subscription model, we must all become more critical consumers of content. We must be aware of the psychological tricks that are used to keep us subscribed, and we must be willing to vote with our wallets. The future of our digital lives may depend on it.

## CONCLUSION

In the end, the Netflix-ification of gaming is a bit like a Las Vegas buffet: it’s a dazzling spectacle of excess that promises endless satisfaction but often leaves you feeling a little bloated and regretful. We’ve journeyed through the psychological minefield of the subscription model, from the paralyzing paradox of choice to the phantom limb syndrome of digital ownership. We’ve seen how services like Xbox Game Pass and PlayStation Plus are not just changing the way we play, but the way we think about play itself. The days of the cherished game cartridge may be over, but the game itself is far from finished. As we navigate this brave new world of endless content, we must remember that the ultimate goal is not to consume as much as possible, but to find genuine joy and meaning in the games we choose to play. So, by all means, enjoy the buffet, but don’t forget to savor the steak.
references:
Kahneman, D., & Tversky, A. (1979). Prospect theory: An analysis of decision under risk. Econometrica, 47(2), 263–291.
Samuelson, W., & Zeckhauser, R. (1988). Status quo bias in decision making. Journal of Risk and Uncertainty, 1(1), 7–59.
Van Crombrugge, M., & Stremersch, S. (2025). The rise of the subscription model in the video game console industry: Unveiling the commercial consequences for platform owners and video game sellers. International Journal of Research in Marketing, 42(4), 1058-1083.
Schwartz, B. (2004). The paradox of choice: Why more is less. Ecco.
